Your job is to CONSOLIDATE and CLEAN the ruleset.

ACTIONS (in priority order):
1. MERGE: Two+ rules with similar/overlapping patterns targeting the same output/label.
   Combine into one rule. Only merge rules of the same format and same output_template/output_key.
2. REMOVE rules that hurt more than they help:
   - precision=0 AND matches>0 (pure noise — every match is wrong)
   - false_positives > 2x true_positives (rule causes more harm than good)
   - Memorized exact strings from training data that won't generalize (e.g. matching one specific phrase)
3. TIGHTEN: If a rule has high FP, return it as a merge-with-self — same rule_id but a narrower pattern.

IMPORTANT — do NOT remove:
- The only rule for a class/label — even if it looks weak, tighten it instead
- Rules with 0 matches — the training set may be small, they could help on unseen data

LOOK FOR:
- Near-duplicate rules (same type, similar regex) — merge them
- Overly broad patterns (like bare \d+ or [A-Z][a-z]+) with high FP — tighten or remove
- Rules that are subsets of other rules (one pattern already covered by another)

Return JSON:
{
  "analysis": "Brief summary (1-2 sentences)",
  "actions": [
    {"action": "merge", "rule_ids": ["id1", "id2"], "merged_pattern": "new regex pattern", "merged_name": "Combined rule name", "reason": "why"},
    {"action": "remove", "rule_ids": ["id"], "reason": "why"}
  ]
}

Return {"analysis": "All rules are useful", "actions": []} if no changes needed.

metadata.analysis: Analysis reveals two primary areas for consolidation: the 'Austrian Courts' rule has a high false positive rate (5 FPs) and should be tightened to exclude partial matches, and the 'Tax Authorities' rule is incomplete (truncated pattern) causing potential issues. No rules meet the strict criteria for removal (precision=0 with matches>0 or FP > 2x TP), but the 'Courts' rule requires immediate tightening to improve precision.

messages.content:
You are updating an existing rule-based extractor. Do NOT rewrite good rules; add or adjust only what is needed.

Task: German Legal Named Entity Recognition
Description: Recognize named entities in German legal text. Entities to look for: organisation.

Input schema: {'text': 'str'}
Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label
        
AVAILABLE ENTITY TYPES: ['organisation']
Rules MUST use one of these types in output_template.


DATA EVIDENCE FROM TRAINING:
- organisation (15 unique): "Verwaltungsgerichtshofes", "Bundesfinanzgericht", "Magistrat der Stadt Wien", "Verwaltungsgerichtshof", "FA Braunau Ried Sch\u00e4rding", "BFG", "B-GmbH", "A-GmbH", "Finanzamt Braunau Ried Sch\u00e4rding", "VwGH", "Bundesfinanzgerichtes", "Hausverwaltung-GmbH", "Finanzamtes Neunkirchen Wr. Neustadt", "Finanzamt \u00d6sterreich", "InnMarine GMBH"
  Exact pattern: (?:F(?:inanzamt(?:(?:es Neunkirchen Wr\. Neustadt| Österreich)| Braunau Ried Schärding)|A Braunau Ried Schärding)|Verwaltungsgerichtshofes|Magistrat der Stadt Wien|Verwaltungsgerichtshof|B(?:undesfinanzgerichtes|FG)|Bundesfinanzgericht|Hausverwaltung\-GmbH|(?:InnMarine GMB|VwG)H|B\-GmbH|A\-GmbH)
  In context: "...Rechtsprechung des [Verwaltungsgerichtshofes] handelt es sich...", "Auch das [Bundesfinanzgericht] folgt dieser...", "...wird auf die  vom [Bundesfinanzgericht] als rechtsrichtig...", "Auch das [Bundesfinanzgericht] folgt...", "...2 BFGG hat das [Bundesfinanzgericht], soweit dies nicht..."

Note: Some entities contain multiple words; do NOT assume single-token matches.

Computed patterns match training strings only; generalize carefully.



CURRENT RULES (full details, note any user_feedback on specific rules):
[
  {
    "name": "AMS Full Name Priority",
    "description": "Matches 'AMS \u00d6sterreich' and 'Arbeitsmarktservice (AMS)' to prevent partial matching of 'AMS' alone when the full name is present.",
    "format": "regex",
    "content": "\\b(?:AMS\\s+\\u00d6sterreich|Arbeitsmarktservice\\s+\\(AMS\\))\\b",
    "priority": 11,
    "output_template": {
      "text": "$0",
      "start": "$start",
      "end": "$end",
      "type": "organisation"
    },
    "output_key": "entities"
  },
  {
    "name": "Social Security & Pension Institutions",
    "description": "Matches Austrian social security and pension institutions including abbreviations and full names.",
    "format": "regex",
    "content": "\\b(?:PVA|SVS/SVB|Pensionsversicherungsanstalt|Sozialversicherungsanstalt der Bauern|Sozialversicherung der Bauern|PSD Wien(?: Ambulatorium Landstra\u00dfe)?|PSD Wien Ambulatorium Landstra\u00dfe)\\b",
    "priority": 10,
    "output_template": {
      "text": "$0",
      "start": "$start",
      "end": "$end",
      "type": "organisation"
    },
    "output_key": "entities"
  },
  {
    "name": "International Organizations",
    "description": "Matches international organization names found in the training data and failure cases.",
    "format": "regex",
    "content": "\\bThe\\s+International\\s+Sivananda\\s+Yoga\\s+Vedanta\\s+Centre\\b",
    "priority": 10,
    "output_template": {
      "text": "$0",
      "start": "$start",
      "end": "$end",
      "type": "organisation"
    },
    "output_key": "entities"
  },
  {
    "name": "Specialized Authorities",
    "description": "Matches 'Amtes f\u00fcr Betrugsbek\u00e4mpfung'.",
    "format": "regex",
    "content": "\\b(?:Amtes\\s+f\\u00fcr\\s+Betrugsbek\u00e4mpfung)\\b",
    "priority": 10,
    "output_template": {
      "text": "$0",
      "start": "$start",
      "end": "$end",
      "type": "organisation"
    },
    "output_key": "entities"

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Your job is to CONSOLIDATE and CLEAN the ruleset.

ACTIONS (in priority order):
1. MERGE: Two+ rules with similar/overlapping patterns targeting the same output/label.
   Combine into one rule. Only merge rules of the same format and same output_template/output_key.
2. REMOVE rules that hurt more than they help:
   - precision=0 AND matches>0 (pure noise — every match is wrong)
   - false_positives > 2x true_positives (rule causes more harm than good)
   - Memorized exact strings from training data that won't generalize (e.g. matching one specific phrase)
3. TIGHTEN: If a rule has high FP, return it as a merge-with-self — same rule_id but a narrower pattern.

IMPORTANT — do NOT remove:
- The only rule for a class/label — even if it looks weak, tighten it instead
- Rules with 0 matches — the training set may be small, they could help on unseen data

LOOK FOR:
- Near-duplicate rules (same type, similar regex) — merge them
- Overly broad patterns (like bare \d+ or [A-Z][a-z]+) with high FP — tighten or remove
- Rules that are subsets of other rules (one pattern already covered by another)

{
  "analysis": "Brief summary (1-2 sentences)",
  "actions": [
    {"action": "merge", "rule_ids": ["id1", "id2"], "merged_pattern": "new regex pattern", "merged_name": "Combined rule name", "reason": "why"},
    {"action": "remove", "rule_ids": ["id"], "reason": "why"}
  ]
}

Return {"analysis": "All rules are useful", "actions": []} if no changes needed.

You are updating an existing rule-based extractor. Do NOT rewrite good rules; add or adjust only what is needed.

Task: German Legal Named Entity Recognition
Description: Recognize named entities in German legal text. Entities to look for: organisation.

Input schema: {'text': 'str'}
Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label
        
AVAILABLE ENTITY TYPES: ['organisation']

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Return JSON:
{
  "analysis": "What patterns did you find? What went wrong in corrections?",
  "strategy": "Overall approach",

      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 1-10 (higher = more important),
      "reasoning": "Why this rule is needed"
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": ["name_of_rule_to_remove (optional, only when replacing a bad rule)"]
}

Your job is to CONSOLIDATE and CLEAN the ruleset.

ACTIONS (in priority order):
1. MERGE: Two+ rules with similar/overlapping patterns targeting the same output/label.
   Combine into one rule. Only merge rules of the same format and same output_template/output_key.
2. REMOVE rules that hurt more than they help:
   - precision=0 AND matches>0 (pure noise — every match is wrong)
   - false_positives > 2x true_positives (rule causes more harm than good)
   - Memorized exact strings from training data that won't generalize (e.g. matching one specific phrase)
3. TIGHTEN: If a rule has high FP, return it as a merge-with-self — same rule_id but a narrower pattern.

IMPORTANT — do NOT remove:
- The only rule for a class/label — even if it looks weak, tighten it instead
- Rules with 0 matches — the training set may be small, they could help on unseen data

LOOK FOR:
- Near-duplicate rules (same type, similar regex) — merge them
- Overly broad patterns (like bare \d+ or [A-Z][a-z]+) with high FP — tighten or remove
- Rules that are subsets of other rules (one pattern already covered by another)

Return JSON:
{
  "analysis": "Brief summary (1-2 sentences)",
  "actions": [
    {"action": "merge", "rule_ids": ["id1", "id2"], "merged_pattern": "new regex pattern", "merged_name": "Combined rule name", "reason": "why"},
    {"action": "remove", "rule_ids": ["id"], "reason": "why"}
  ]
}

Return {"analysis": "All rules are useful", "actions": []} if no changes needed.

metadata.analysis: Analysis reveals significant redundancy in tax authority and ministry rules, with several rules targeting overlapping entities (e.g., 'Finanzamt' variations and 'Bundesministerium' genitive forms). Additionally, specific entity rules with very low recall (e.g., 'Österreichische Gesellschaft für Europapolitik', 'BM für Finanzen') are likely overfitting to the training set and should be consolidated into broader, more generalizable patterns.

messages.content:
You are updating an existing rule-based extractor. Do NOT rewrite good rules; add or adjust only what is needed.

Task: German Legal Named Entity Recognition
Description: Recognize named entities in German legal text. Entities to look for: organisation.

Input schema: {'text': 'str'}
Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label
        
AVAILABLE ENTITY TYPES: ['organisation']
Rules MUST use one of these types in output_template.


DATA EVIDENCE FROM TRAINING:
- organisation (15 unique): "Verwaltungsgerichtshof", "Unter Donunisee AG", "Bundesfinanzgericht", "Bundesfinanzgerichtes", "Verwaltungsgerichtshofes", "Mag. Ghesla Steuerberater GmbH", "Finanzamtes \u00d6sterreich", "Schweizerischen Unfallversicherungsanstalt (SUVA)", "SUVA", "\u00d6GK", "X GmbH", "Bundesministerium f\u00fcr Finanzen", "Bundesministerin f\u00fcr Finanzen", "Finanzamtes Wien 1/23", "Finanzamt \u00d6sterreich"
  Exact pattern: (?:S(?:chweizerischen Unfallversicherungsanstalt \(SUVA\)|UVA)|Bundesministeri(?:um|n) für Finanzen|Mag\. Ghesla Steuerberater GmbH|Verwaltungsgerichtshofes|Verwaltungsgerichtshof|Finanzamt(?:es (?:Österreich|Wien 1/23)| Österreich)|Bundesfinanzgerichtes|Bundesfinanzgericht|Unter Donunisee AG|X GmbH|ÖGK)
  In context: "...Revision an den [Verwaltungsgerichtshof] nach Art. 133 Abs....", "...vom  Arbeitgeber [Unter Donunisee AG]  ber\u00fccksichtigt...", "...bei der Fa. [Unter Donunisee AG]  in Ansatz...", "...der Firma [Unter Donunisee AG]  vom 09.03.2021,...", "... durch das [Bundesfinanzgericht] (=\u201cVorlageantrag\u201c)..."

Note: Some entities contain multiple words; do NOT assume single-token matches.

Computed patterns match training strings only; generalize carefully.



CURRENT RULES (full details, note any user_feedback on specific rules):

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

   - false_positives > 2x true_positives (rule causes more harm than good)
   - Memorized exact strings from training data that won't generalize (e.g. matching one specific phrase)
3. TIGHTEN: If a rule has high FP, return it as a merge-with-self — same rule_id but a narrower pattern.

IMPORTANT — do NOT remove:
- The only rule for a class/label — even if it looks weak, tighten it instead
- Rules with 0 matches — the training set may be small, they could help on unseen data

LOOK FOR:
- Near-duplicate rules (same type, similar regex) — merge them
- Overly broad patterns (like bare \d+ or [A-Z][a-z]+) with high FP — tighten or remove
- Rules that are subsets of other rules (one pattern already covered by another)

Return JSON:
{
  "analysis": "Brief summary (1-2 sentences)",
  "actions": [
    {"action": "merge", "rule_ids": ["id1", "id2"], "merged_pattern": "new regex pattern", "merged_name": "Combined rule name", "reason": "why"},
    {"action": "remove", "rule_ids": ["id"], "reason": "why"}
  ]
}

Return {"analysis": "All rules are useful", "actions": []} if no changes needed.

metadata.analysis: Analysis reveals significant overlap between rules targeting Austrian Federal Ministries (specifically BM für Finanzen and BM für Inneres) and their genitive forms, as well as a high false-positive rate for the generic 'X GmbH' placeholder rule. The 'Austrian Courts' rule has the highest volume of matches but suffers from low recall, suggesting it needs expansion rather than removal. No rules have 0 precision with matches > 0, so no immediate 'pure noise' removals are required, but the Ministry rules should be consolidated to improve maintainability and coverage.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 8.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Return JSON:

      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 1-10 (higher = more important),
      "reasoning": "Why this rule is needed"
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": ["name_of_rule_to_remove (optional, only when replacing a bad rule)"]
}

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Your job is to CONSOLIDATE and CLEAN the ruleset.

ACTIONS (in priority order):
1. MERGE: Two+ rules with similar/overlapping patterns targeting the same output/label.
   Combine into one rule. Only merge rules of the same format and same output_template/output_key.
2. REMOVE rules that hurt more than they help:
   - precision=0 AND matches>0 (pure noise — every match is wrong)
   - false_positives > 2x true_positives (rule causes more harm than good)
   - Memorized exact strings from training data that won't generalize (e.g. matching one specific phrase)
3. TIGHTEN: If a rule has high FP, return it as a merge-with-self — same rule_id but a narrower pattern.

IMPORTANT — do NOT remove:
- The only rule for a class/label — even if it looks weak, tighten it instead
- Rules with 0 matches — the training set may be small, they could help on unseen data

LOOK FOR:
- Near-duplicate rules (same type, similar regex) — merge them
- Overly broad patterns (like bare \d+ or [A-Z][a-z]+) with high FP — tighten or remove
- Rules that are subsets of other rules (one pattern already covered by another)

Return JSON:
{
  "analysis": "Brief summary (1-2 sentences)",
  "actions": [
    {"action": "merge", "rule_ids": ["id1", "id2"], "merged_pattern": "new regex pattern", "merged_name": "Combined rule name", "reason": "why"},
    {"action": "remove", "rule_ids": ["id"], "reason": "why"}
  ]
}

Return {"analysis": "All rules are useful", "actions": []} if no changes needed.

metadata.analysis: Analysis reveals one critical rule ('Generic GmbH/AG Companies') with a high false positive rate (7 FPs vs 4 TPs) that is overly broad and should be removed as it degrades overall precision. Additionally, two rules targeting 'Tax Authorities' and 'Social Security' institutions have 0 matches and appear to be subsets or redundant with broader patterns or specific entity rules, but since they have 0 matches, they are retained per instructions. No other rules meet the strict removal criteria (precision=0 with matches>0 or FP > 2x TP).

You are updating an existing rule-based extractor. Do NOT rewrite good rules; add or adjust only what is needed.

Task: German Legal Named Entity Recognition
Description: Recognize named entities in German legal text. Entities to look for: organisation.

Input schema: {'text': 'str'}
Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label
        
AVAILABLE ENTITY TYPES: ['organisation']
Rules MUST use one of these types in output_template.


DATA EVIDENCE FROM TRAINING:
- organisation (15 unique): "Houdek Maschinenbau", "VwGH", "Roelfsen Versicherung", "Dorfcon-Verlag", "Schmeltz Luftfahrt", "BMF", "FA Wien 1/23", "UFS", "BFG, Au\u00dfenstelle Linz", "BFG", "Lexdon IT", "Bundesfinanzgerichts, Au\u00dfenstelle Linz", "BFG (Au\u00dfenstelle Linz)", "Finanzamt Wien 1/23", "Bundesfinanzgerichtes"
  Exact pattern: (?:B(?:undesfinanzgericht(?:s, Außenstelle Linz|es)|FG \(Außenstelle Linz\)|FG, Außenstelle Linz|MF)|Roelfsen Versicherung|F(?:inanzamt|A) Wien 1/23|Houdek Maschinenbau|Schmeltz Luftfahrt|Dorfcon\-Verlag|Lexdon IT|VwGH|BFG|UFS)
  In context: "...aus,  dass die [Houdek Maschinenbau]  aufgrund der...", "...im Sinne der [VwGH]- Rechtsprechung...", "...sei die [VwGH]- Rechtsprechung...", "... daher zwischen [Roelfsen Versicherung]  und...", "...Versicherung  und [Dorfcon-Verlag]  wie folgt aliquot..."

Note: Some entities contain multiple words; do NOT assume single-token matches.

Computed patterns match training strings only; generalize carefully.

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

}

Task: German Legal Named Entity Recognition
Description: Recognize named entities in German legal text. Entities to look for: organisation.

Input schema: {'text': 'str'}
Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label
        
AVAILABLE ENTITY TYPES: ['organisation']
Rules MUST use one of these types in output_template.


DATA EVIDENCE FROM TRAINING:
- organisation (12 unique): "Houdek Maschinenbau", "BFG", "Roelfsen Versicherung", "Finanzamt Wien 1/23", "VwGH", "UFS", "FA Wien 1/23", "Verwaltungsgerichtshof", "Bundesfinanzgerichtes", "Verfassungsgerichtshof", "VfGH", "Verwaltungsgerichtshofes"
  Exact pattern: (?:V(?:er(?:waltungsgerichtshofes|fassungsgerichtshof)|[fw]GH)|Verwaltungsgerichtshof|Bundesfinanzgerichtes|Roelfsen Versicherung|F(?:inanzamt|A) Wien 1/23|Houdek Maschinenbau|BFG|UFS)
  In context: "Der bei  der [Houdek Maschinenbau]  im Jahr 2007...", "Rechtsansicht  des [BFG] und der...", "...\u00fcbrigen bei  der [Houdek Maschinenbau]  verbliebenen...", "...Verlusten) bei der [Roelfsen Versicherung]  f\u00fchrt.", "...hat daher das [BFG] \u00fcber die H\u00f6he  des..."

Note: Some entities contain multiple words; do NOT assume single-token matches.

Computed patterns match training strings only; generalize carefully.



CURRENT RULES (full details, note any user_feedback on specific rules):

- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.

- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

Return JSON:
{
  "analysis": "What patterns did you find? What went wrong in corrections?",
  "strategy": "Overall approach",
  "note": "Every rule must include both output_template and output_key",
  "rules": [
    {
      "name": "Short rule name",
      "description": "What this rule does",
      "format": "regex",
      "pattern": "regex pattern",
      "output_template": {"text": "$0", "start": "$start", "end": "$end", "type": "LITERAL_OR_$VAR"},
      "output_key": "entities",
      "priority": 1-10 (higher = more important),
      "reasoning": "Why this rule is needed"
    }
  ],
  "deleted_rules": ["name_of_rule_to_remove (optional, only when replacing a bad rule)"]
}

Your job is to CONSOLIDATE and CLEAN the ruleset.

ACTIONS (in priority order):
1. MERGE: Two+ rules with similar/overlapping patterns targeting the same output/label.
   Combine into one rule. Only merge rules of the same format and same output_template/output_key.
2. REMOVE rules that hurt more than they help:
   - precision=0 AND matches>0 (pure noise — every match is wrong)
   - false_positives > 2x true_positives (rule causes more harm than good)
   - Memorized exact strings from training data that won't generalize (e.g. matching one specific phrase)
3. TIGHTEN: If a rule has high FP, return it as a merge-with-self — same rule_id but a narrower pattern.

IMPORTANT — do NOT remove:
- The only rule for a class/label — even if it looks weak, tighten it instead
- Rules with 0 matches — the training set may be small, they could help on unseen data

LOOK FOR:
- Near-duplicate rules (same type, similar regex) — merge them
- Overly broad patterns (like bare \d+ or [A-Z][a-z]+) with high FP — tighten or remove
- Rules that are subsets of other rules (one pattern already covered by another)

Return JSON:
{
  "analysis": "Brief summary (1-2 sentences)",
  "actions": [
    {"action": "merge", "rule_ids": ["id1", "id2"], "merged_pattern": "new regex pattern", "merged_name": "Combined rule name", "reason": "why"},
    {"action": "remove", "rule_ids": ["id"], "reason": "why"}
  ]
}

Return {"analysis": "All rules are useful", "actions": []} if no changes needed.

metadata.analysis: Analysis reveals two primary issues: 1) The 'Austrian Courts' rule (d5623957) has a high false positive rate (15 FPs vs 39 TPs) and a truncated pattern, requiring tightening. 2) The 'Law Firms' rule (0aa96796) is truncated and incomplete, likely missing the closing parenthesis or pattern logic, which should be merged with the specific 'Lenfeld/Leys/Sonderegger' rule (2a9f6d02) to ensure completeness, or the truncated rule should be fixed. However, since the 'Law Firms' rule is truncated in the provided JSON (missing closing `\b` or closing parenthesis for the group), it is safer to treat it as a candidate for removal or strict fixing. Upon closer inspection, the 'Law Firms' rule pattern ends abruptly at `Rechtsanw\u00e4l` without a closing word boundary or group closure, making it syntactically invalid or incomplete in this context. The 'Courts' rule is the most critical to fix due to high FP. The 'Roelfsen', 'Houdek', and 'Tax Authorities' rules have high precision but low recall; they are kept as they are specific entities. The 'Law Firms' rule is removed due to being syntactically broken (truncated) in the input, which would cause errors or no matches.

You are updating an existing rule-based extractor. Do NOT rewrite good rules; add or adjust only what is needed.

Task: German Legal Named Entity Recognition
Description: Recognize named entities in German legal text. Entities to look for: organisation.

Input schema: {'text': 'str'}
Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label
        
AVAILABLE ENTITY TYPES: ['organisation']
Rules MUST use one of these types in output_template.


DATA EVIDENCE FROM TRAINING:
- organisation (15 unique): "Houdek Maschinenbau", "Schmeltz Luftfahrt", "Dorfcon-Verlag", "Lexdon IT", "Roelfsen Versicherung", "Betriebspr\u00fcfung GmbH", "Bundesfinanzgericht", "Bundesfinanzgerichtes", "Verwaltungsgerichtshof", "VwGH", "BFG", "Verfassungsgerichtshofes", "FA Wien 1/23", "Finanzamt Wien 1/23", "Verwaltungsgerichtshofes"
  Exact pattern: (?:V(?:er(?:walt|fass)ungsgerichtshofes|wGH)|Verwaltungsgerichtshof|B(?:undesfinanzgerichtes|(?:etriebsprüfung GmbH|FG))|Roelfsen Versicherung|Bundesfinanzgericht|F(?:inanzamt|A) Wien 1/23|Houdek Maschinenbau|Schmeltz Luftfahrt|Dorfcon\-Verlag|Lexdon IT)
  In context: "...Jahr 2007 hat die [Houdek Maschinenbau]  insgesamt 84...", "...auf die [Schmeltz Luftfahrt]  nach Art VI...", "Die [Schmeltz Luftfahrt]  wurde in weiterer...", "...Folge mit der  [Dorfcon-Verlag]  verschmolzen.", "Die [Houdek Maschinenbau]  selbst wurde..."

Note: Some entities contain multiple words; do NOT assume single-token matches.

Computed patterns match training strings only; generalize carefully.



CURRENT RULES (full details, note any user_feedback on specific rules):

Instructions:
- Add, tweak, or DELETE rules to fix the shown failures and reduce false positives.
- Pay close attention to user_feedback on rules AND task-level USER GUIDANCE — these are direct instructions from the user and MUST be addressed even if there are no failures.
- If a rule has user_feedback, modify or replace that rule to address the feedback.
- IMPORTANT: When updating an existing rule, you MUST reuse the EXACT same "name" as the original rule. Do NOT add suffixes like "_fixed", "_v2", "_updated", etc. The merge system uses name-matching to replace the old version — a different name creates a duplicate instead of replacing.
- If a rule is fundamentally too broad (FP >> TP) and you're providing better, narrower replacements, list the old rule's exact name in "deleted_rules". Only delete if you're providing replacements in "rules".
- If a rule has high false positives (FP >> TP), TIGHTEN its pattern or DELETE it and add narrower replacements. Adding context or narrowing the match is better than piling on new rules.
- Use structural patterns with word boundaries that generalize to unseen text.
- Keep total new/updated rules <= 30.
- Use formats: regex
- Avoid touching unrelated behaviors.

WHAT MAKES A GOOD RULE:
- PRECISION OVER RECALL: A rule that matches 10 things correctly beats one that matches 100 with 20 wrong. Never sacrifice precision for recall. Missing a match is fixable later; a wrong match poisons results.
- GENERALIZE, DON'T MEMORIZE: Rules run on unseen text. Match the *structure* of values, not the exact strings you see in training data.
- USE CONTEXT: The same string can mean different things. Match surrounding words/punctuation and use capture groups ($1) to extract just the value.
- ONE RULE, ONE PATTERN: Multiple focused rules beat one giant regex. Each rule should have a clear, testable reason to exist.
- THINK ADVERSARIALLY: Before writing a rule, ask "what else could this match?" If the answer isn't empty, narrow the pattern or add context.


- Regex patterns (for structured extraction)

IMPORTANT: You must ONLY use the allowed formats listed above. Do NOT generate rules in other formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include Python/code rules. Only return the listed formats.
IMPORTANT: Do NOT include spaCy rules. Only return the listed formats.

REGEX SYNTAX REFERENCE:
- \b word boundaries, (?:...) non-capturing groups, (a|b|c) alternation
- [A-Z] [a-z] \d \s character classes, +*?{n,m} quantifiers
- (?=...) lookahead, (?<=...) lookbehind — match context without consuming it
- (?i) case-insensitive flag

CAPTURE GROUPS ($1) vs FULL MATCH ($0):
- Use $0 in output_template when the entire regex match IS the value you want.
- Use $1 when you need surrounding context to disambiguate — put the value in a capture group.
  With "text": "$1", $start/$end auto-adjust to the group position automatically.

GOOD vs BAD PATTERNS (learn from these):

  BAD:  "\d+"                       — matches every number in the text, most will be wrong type
  GOOD: "(\d+)\s*(?:kg|lbs|oz)"    — only numbers followed by weight units; $1 captures the number

  BAD:  "Alice|Bob|Tokyo"            — memorizes training strings, misses all unseen values
  GOOD: "(?:Mr|Mrs|Dr)\.?\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — captures names after titles

  BAD:  "[A-Z][a-z]+"               — matches any capitalized word (way too broad)
  GOOD: "(?:in|from|at|near)\s+([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)" — locations after prepositions

  BAD:  "\b\w+@\w+\.\w+\b"         — matches email-like strings but also "user@v1.0"
  GOOD: "(?:email|contact)[:\s]+(\S+@\S+\.\w{2,})" — emails after a label; $1 captures address

  BAD:  one giant regex that handles 5 different sub-patterns
  GOOD: 5 separate rules, each handling one clear case with its own priority

IMPORTANT:
- Do NOT assume entities are capitalized; check the training data.
- Prefer structural patterns (\d+, [A-Z][a-z]+) over memorized alternations.
- When multiple entity types share similar text, use surrounding context to disambiguate.




SCHEMA-AWARE RULES:
For this task, rules must include an output_template that maps matches to the output schema.

Output schema:
entities: List[Entity]

Entity:
  text: string  # The matched text span
  start: integer  # Start character offset
  end: integer  # End character offset
  type: string  # Entity label

Each rule needs:
- pattern: The regex pattern to match
- output_template: JSON template for each match, using variables:
  - $0: Full match text
  - $start: Start character offset
  - $end: End character offset
  - $1, $2, ...: Capture groups (regex only)
- output_key: Which output array to populate (e.g., "entities")

$0 vs $1: Use $0 when the full match IS the value. Use a capture group ($1) when you
need surrounding context for disambiguation — $start/$end auto-adjust to the group.
  Example: "prefix(\w+)" with "text": "$1" extracts only the captured part.

metadata.analysis: The 'Austrian Courts' rule (11dd14d6) has a high false positive rate (11 FPs) and appears to be cut off mid-pattern ('Arbeit'), likely causing noise; it should be tightened. The 'Tax Authorities' (dbf1596c) and 'Organizations' (e8f383b9) rules have very low recall but perfect precision, indicating they are valid but incomplete; they should be kept as-is or potentially merged if they share a common 'Organisation' label, but given the specific nature of the patterns, they are best left alone to avoid over-generalization. The other rules have 0 matches, so they are retained as they may be useful for unseen data. No rules meet the strict criteria for removal (precision=0 with matches>0 or FP > 2x TP).

  "analysis": "The provided training data and failure logs indicate a critical gap in recognizing specific educational institutions: 'London Film Academy' and its misspelled variant 'London Film Acadamy'. The existing rules cover Austrian entities (courts, ministries, specific companies) but completely miss these UK-based entities. The failures show that the system fails to extract 'London Film Academy', 'London Film  Acadamy' (with double space), and 'London Film Acadamy' (with single space). The current 'Education Institutions Consolidated' rule does not include these names. Additionally, the 'Bundeskanzleramtes' entity is failing, likely due to the specific genitive form or spacing not being covered by the generic 'Bundesministerium' rules which focus on 'Bundesministeriums' or 'Bundesministerin'.",
  "strategy": "1. Add a specific rule for 'London Film Academy' and 'London Film Acadamy' to handle the spelling variations and spacing issues found in the failures. This rule will use a flexible pattern to match 'Acad[ae]my' and allow for variable whitespace. 2. Add a specific rule for 'Bundeskanzleramtes' to ensure the genitive form is captured, as the existing 'Tax Authorities & Federal Ministries' rule focuses on 'Bundesministeriums' (nominative/accusative) and 'Bundesministerin' but misses the specific 'Bundeskanzleramtes' form. 3. Keep all existing rules as they are functioning correctly for their respective domains (Austrian courts, specific companies, etc.).",

    }
  ],

}

      "recall": 0.12,
      "f1": 0.22,
      "true_positives": 1,
      "false_positives": 0
    }

Your job is to CONSOLIDATE and CLEAN the ruleset.

ACTIONS (in priority order):
1. MERGE: Two+ rules with similar/overlapping patterns targeting the same output/label.
   Combine into one rule. Only merge rules of the same format and same output_template/output_key.
2. REMOVE rules that hurt more than they help:
   - precision=0 AND matches>0 (pure noise — every match is wrong)
   - false_positives > 2x true_positives (rule causes more harm than good)
   - Memorized exact strings from training data that won't generalize (e.g. matching one specific phrase)
3. TIGHTEN: If a rule has high FP, return it as a merge-with-self — same rule_id but a narrower pattern.

IMPORTANT — do NOT remove:
- The only rule for a class/label — even if it looks weak, tighten it instead
- Rules with 0 matches — the training set may be small, they could help on unseen data

LOOK FOR:
- Near-duplicate rules (same type, similar regex) — merge them
- Overly broad patterns (like bare \d+ or [A-Z][a-z]+) with high FP — tighten or remove
- Rules that are subsets of other rules (one pattern already covered by another)

Return JSON:
{
  "analysis": "Brief summary (1-2 sentences)",
  "actions": [
    {"action": "merge", "rule_ids": ["id1", "id2"], "merged_pattern": "new regex pattern", "merged_name": "Combined rule name", "reason": "why"},
    {"action": "remove", "rule_ids": ["id"], "reason": "why"}
  ]
}

Return {"analysis": "All rules are useful", "actions": []} if no changes needed.
